topic: The People's Health c1250 to Present
subject: history

items.q: Did medieval towns make no effort at public health?
items.correct: No - although they lacked germ theory, towns passed rules about latrines, waste disposal and butchers' offal; the problem was weak enforcement, not total ignorance.
items.misconception: Medieval people did not care about dirt and made no public health rules at all.
items.random_wrong: Medieval towns had modern sewer systems built by the Romans for them.
items.why: The 'filthy Middle Ages' stereotype hides real civic efforts; examiners reward knowing the rules existed but were hard to enforce.

items.q: Was miasma theory completely useless for public health?
items.correct: It was wrong about causes, but cleaning up the smells it blamed meant removing real filth and sewage, so reforms driven by miasma still improved health.
items.misconception: Because miasma theory was wrong, the reforms it inspired achieved nothing.
items.random_wrong: Miasma theory was a method of vaccinating against disease.
items.why: Students assume wrong theory means wasted effort; right action for the wrong reason is a recurring pattern in the history of public health.

items.q: Did the 1848 Public Health Act force every town to clean up?
items.correct: No - the 1848 Act was largely permissive, letting towns set up health boards if they chose; the 1875 Act made key duties compulsory.
items.misconception: The 1848 Public Health Act compelled all towns to build sewers and supply clean water.
items.random_wrong: The 1848 Act abolished local councils in Britain.
items.why: Confusing the permissive 1848 Act with the compulsory 1875 Act loses a key mark; the difference shows how slowly government accepted responsibility.

items.q: How did London's authorities respond to plague in 1665?
items.correct: Infected households were shut up and marked with a red cross
items.misconception: The population was vaccinated against plague
items.random_wrong: A citywide network of sewers was built immediately
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Infected households were shut up and marked with a red cross. “The population was vaccinated against plague” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did John Snow demonstrate in 1854?
items.correct: That cholera was spread through contaminated water, by mapping cases around the Broad Street pump
items.misconception: That cholera was spread by bad smells in the air
items.random_wrong: That plague was carried by rat fleas
items.why: It's That cholera was spread through contaminated water, by mapping cases around the Broad Street pump. “That cholera was spread by bad smells in the air” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did the 1875 Public Health Act do?
items.correct: Made it compulsory for councils to provide clean water, sewers and a medical officer of health
items.random_wrong: Banned all factory work for children
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Made it compulsory for councils to provide clean water, sewers and a medical officer of health. “Created the National Health Service” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On what principle was the NHS founded in 1948?
items.correct: Healthcare free at the point of use, funded by taxation
items.misconception: Healthcare only for those with insurance
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Healthcare free at the point of use, funded by taxation. “Healthcare only for those with insurance” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Black Death reach England?
items.why: Exact answer: 1348 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
